title: Microservices Principles & DDD
learningObjectives: Explain Microservices Principles & DDD with phase-6 depth and clarity
Implement Java examples for Microservices Principles & DDD with production awareness
Identify failure risks and mitigation strategies
Reason about trade-offs under realistic constraints
Design or optimize solutions with maintainability in mind
Present interview answers with concrete evidence and structure

sections.title: Why Microservices Principles & DDD matters
sections.content:
Microservices Principles & DDD is a high-value Day 51 topic in Phase 6 (REST APIs and microservice communication trade-offs).

At this stage, interviewers evaluate whether you can connect implementation details with reliability, scalability, and maintainability outcomes.

What strong answers include:
1. precise concept framing,
2. operational implications,
3. one realistic failure pattern,
4. one practical mitigation.

This topic is not just about code that compiles. It is about decisions that survive production traffic and team ownership.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Microservices Principles & DDD
sections.content:
### Day 51: Microservices Principles & DDD
Phase context: REST APIs and microservice communication trade-offs.

### What to understand deeply
- mechanism and boundaries
- runtime behavior and cost
- operational failure modes
- trade-offs vs alternatives

### Implementation workflow
1. Build a clear baseline solution.
2. Validate edge/failure paths.
3. Measure impact (latency, memory, throughput, or complexity).
4. Refactor for readability and maintainability.

### Interview framing template
- "The problem this solves is..."
- "I chose this approach because..."
- "The key trade-off is..."
- "A failure case is... and mitigation is..."

### Production perspective
For Microservices Principles & DDD, quality is judged by correctness + resilience + debuggability, not just API usage.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**API Gateway** handles auth, rate limit, routing, TLS termination — keep domain services simple behind it.

**Service discovery:** DNS/K8s services vs Eureka — health-aware load balancing.

**Circuit breaker** (Resilience4j) — fail fast when dependency sick; bulkhead isolates thread pools.

**Timeouts everywhere** — client connect/read; default infinite hangs cascade failures.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: DDD: aggregate root wraps invariants; only root is referenced externally.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Bounded context: separate modules with explicit anti-corruption translation.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Domain event: immutable past fact emitted after state change.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Designing APIs without idempotency and failure semantics.
Coupling services tightly through synchronous chains.
Ignoring consistency boundaries in distributed workflows.
Treating microservice decomposition as purely technical refactor.

sections.title: Exercise - Microservices Principles & DDD Applied Scenario
sections.problem:
Implement a realistic solution for **Microservices Principles & DDD**.

Requirements:
1. Define assumptions and constraints first.
2. Include one failure-path handling strategy.
3. Provide one improvement over baseline.
4. State complexity or runtime impact.
5. Keep implementation review-friendly.
hints: Add one observability/debug aid in output or comments.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is DDD strategic design?
conceptual.answer: Bounded contexts, ubiquitous language, context maps—align business and software boundaries.

conceptual.question: What is aggregate?
conceptual.answer: Cluster of entities with invariant root—transactions scoped to aggregate.

conceptual.question: What is domain event?
conceptual.answer: Something happened in business—past tense facts—drive workflows.

conceptual.question: What is anti-corruption layer?
conceptual.answer: Translate foreign model to your domain—integration boundary.

conceptual.question: What is ubiquitous language?
conceptual.answer: Shared terms devs and domain experts use—reduces mistranslation bugs.

conceptual.question: What is bounded context?
conceptual.answer: Autonomous model boundary—microservice often maps 1:1 not always.

conceptual.question: What is CQRS relation to DDD?
conceptual.answer: Separate read/write models—often paired—not mandatory for DDD.

conceptual.question: What is value object vs entity?
conceptual.answer: Immutable identity-less vs mutable identity-tracked—design clarity.

conceptual.question: What is repository pattern?
conceptual.answer: Domain interface to retrieve aggregates—persistence behind abstraction.

conceptual.question: What is factory in DDD?
conceptual.answer: Complex aggregate creation enforcing invariants at birth.

conceptual.question: What is domain service?
conceptual.answer: Logic not belonging to single entity—still domain layer.

conceptual.question: What is application service?
conceptual.answer: Orchestrate use cases—transaction boundaries often here.

conceptual.question: What is anemic domain model anti-pattern?
conceptual.answer: Entities only getters/setters—behavior in services—lose OO benefits.

conceptual.question: What is event storming workshop?
conceptual.answer: Collaborative discovery of events and aggregates—process technique.

codeBased.question: Anti-corruption PaymentGatewayAdapter
codeBased.answer: Maps Stripe DTO to domain Payment.

seniorScenario.question: Microservice per entity CRUD—problem?
seniorScenario.answer: Wrong bounded context slicing—chatty coupling distributed monolith.

seniorScenario.question: Shared database across contexts—issue?
seniorScenario.answer: Coupling—leaks models; prefer integration events or APIs.

seniorScenario.question: Ubiquitous language drift over years?
seniorScenario.answer: Glossary documentation, code reviews align naming.

seniorScenario.question: Aggregates too large—performance?
seniorScenario.answer: Split reevaluate boundaries; eventual consistency between aggregates.

wrongAnswers: An aggregate can span multiple databases easily.
Domain events must always be implemented with Kafka.
Value objects must always map to database tables.
Anti-corruption layer means rejecting all external data.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Phase 6 Expectation | Quick Rule | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Framing | Explain intent + constraints | Start from problem | API-only answer |
| Design | State trade-off explicitly | Compare alternatives | One-solution bias |
| Runtime | Mention cost impact | Time + space/ops | No performance reasoning |
| Failure | Name one incident pattern | Mitigation path | Happy-path only thinking |
| Communication | Be concise + concrete | Use one example | Generic buzzwords |
